content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assurance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

February 7, 1983

Fred Lowell
Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro
225 Bush Street
San Francisco, CA 94120

Dear Fred:

This is in response to your letter dated December 22, 1982
requesting advice regarding the proper method of reporting certain
payments made by employers of lobbyists and "$2,500 filers"
pursuant to Government Code Section 86109.

Because you did not ask this question on behalf a specifically
identified client, this letter does not constitute advice under
Section 83114 of the Government Code.

In your letter you asked how a lobbyist employer should report
payments to reimburse an employee who spent 10% or more of his
compensated time in an attempt to influence the passage of an
Assembly bill. The reimbursement payments covered such items as
meals and travel expenses incurred by the employee in his attempts
to influence the bill's passage.

Three possible methods of reporting such reimbursement on a
Lobbyist Employer Form 650 as presented in your letter are:

1. Report the payments in Section B (1) as payments to
influence legislative or administrative action outside
the filer's organization; or

2. Report the payments in Section B (3) as part of the
employees "compensation" attributable to attempting to
influence legislative or administrative action; or

3. Report the payments in Section B (3) as other overhead
incurred by the filer.

page 2
Fred Lowell


The Act is not specific regarding the proper method of reporting
such payments. With regard to previous reporting, any of the
three methods of reporting which you describe in your letter are
considered to be adequate.

The staff has recently determined, however, that the proper method
as shown on page 20 of the "Information Manual on Lobbying
Disclosure Provisions" of reporting reimbursement of expenses of
employees who spend 10% or more of their time in lobbying
activities is to report such payments in Section 3, Part 1,
"Payments Aggregating $25 or more outside the Filers
Organization," since the cost were incurred outside of the filers
organization.

In addition to reporting reimbursement of expenses of employees
who spend 10% or more of their time, reimbursement of expenses of
employees who spend less than 10% of their time must also be
reported pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18620 (d)(C), in
the same manner.

Thank you for your inquiry. Please contact me if you have any
additional questions.

Yours Very Truly,

Lynn Montgomery
Political Reform Consultant

WRITER'S DIRECT DIAL NUMBER

(415) 983-1585

TELEPHONE (415) 983-1000

WASHINGTON, D.C.
1527 SEVENTEENTH ST., N.W.
WASHINGTON, D.C. 20036
TELEPHONE 202-887-0303

LOS ANGELES
700 SOUTH FLOWER STREET
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017
TELEPHONE (213) 629-2800

SAN JOSE
100 PARK CENTER PLAZA
SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA 95113
TELEPHONE (408) 287-2233

December 22, 1982

Political Reform Act of 1974 -
Lobbying Activities


Ms. Lynn Montgomery
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Lynn:

     This letter constitutes a request for technical
advice with respect to the proper method of reporting reim-
bursement of expenses by lobbyist-employers and $2,500
filers on their quarterly lobbying reports (Form 650).

     Although it is clear that all expenses associated
with attempts to influence legislative or administrative
action in California must be reported by lobbyist-employers
and $2,500 filers, a question arises as to the proper method
for reporting some items. In order to illustrate my ques-
tion, I offer the following hypothetical:

     The Wigex Corporation files quarterly re-
ports as a lobbyist-employer pursuant to the
lobbying disclosure provisions of the Politi-
cal Reform Act of 1974. It reports the fees
it pays to its lobbyists on Part II(A) of
Form 650 and it reports expenses reimbursed to
its lobbyists on Part II(B). In addition to
reportable activities by its lobbyists, however,
Wigex also employs nonlobbyist employees who
engage in attempts to influence legislative or
administrative action. Occasionally, such

employees are reimbursed for their expenses
in connection with such activity. Such ex-
penses usually take the form of dinners,
rental car reimbursements and the like.

In the fourth quarter of 1982, A. W.
Horton, a Wigex nonlobbyist employee travels
to Sacramento in an attempt to influence the
passage of an Assembly Bill. The Wigex
Corporation reimburses him for the use of his
personal automobile ($40), a meal at The Toxic
Oyster ($30) and a bridge toll paid to the
Big Bridge Transit District ($1). During the
fourth quarter of 1982, Mr. Horton spends more
than 10% of his compensated time on lobbying
activities so that an allocable portion of his
compensation for this period is reportable on
Part III(B)(3) of Form 650. The Wigex Corpora-
tion must also report the expenses incurred
by Mr. Horton during his trip to Sacramento,
but it is unclear where on Form 650 the total
should be entered.

As we have discussed, there are three possible
methods of reporting Mr. Horton's expenses. The first
choice is to report them under Section B(1) as a payment
to influence legislative or administrative action outside
the filer's organization. In this case, the first two
expenses (the cost of the automobile and the dinner) would
be itemized with Mr. Horton as the payee. The third ex-
pense (the bridge toll) would be reported as a nonitemized
payment aggregating less than $25 during the period. The
second alternative would be to simply add the total amount
of the reimbursement to Mr. Horton's compensation since
employment contract, either impliedly or expressly, provides
that he shall be reimbursed for reasonable and necessary
expenses incurred during the course of his employment. The
final alternative would be to report these expenses under
"Other Overhead Incurred by the Filer" (Section B(3)).

There is very little guidance as to the proper
method of reporting these expenses, partly because none of
the categories completely fit the practicalities of the
situation. These expenses are not technically payments
outside the employer's organization since they are made to
an employee, they are not strictly "compensation" although
they could be construed as such, and they do not easily fit
under the category of "overhead."

Naturally, any combination of the three alterna-
tives is possible. Thus, for example, the Commission could

2.

take the position that Mr. Horton's automobile expense
should be added to his compensation, the meal should be
itemized with the name of the restaurant as payee and the
toll should be treated as overhead. However, for the sake
of simplicity, it would be preferable if one alternative
were selected, or failing that, a new reporting category
were added to Form 650.

     In any case, it is my understanding from our
conversations that because of the confusion that has been
generated, the use of any one of these three methods of
reporting these expenses in the past would be considered
by the Commission to be appropriate.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        [signature]

                                        Frederick K. Lowell




3.

sections.question: How should a lobbyist employer report reimbursement of expenses paid to employees who spent 10% or more of their compensated time in attempts to influence legislative or administrative action? Should such expenses be reported in Section B(1) as payments to influence legislative action outside the organization, in Section B(3) as part of employee compensation, or in Section B(3) as overhead?
sections.conclusion: The Act is not specific regarding the proper method of reporting such payments. With regard to previous reporting, any of the three methods of reporting which you describe in your letter are considered to be adequate. The staff has recently determined, however, that the proper method as shown on page 20 of the "Information Manual on Lobbying Disclosure Provisions" of reporting reimbursement of expenses of employees who spend 10% or more of their time in lobbying activities is to report such payments in Section B, Part 1, "Payments Aggregating $25 or more outside the Filers Organization," since the costs were incurred outside of the filers organization. In addition to reporting reimbursement of expenses of employees who spend 10% or more of their time, reimbursement of expenses of employees who spend less than 10% of their time must also be reported pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18620(d)(C), in the same manner.
sections.question_synthetic: The inquirer seeks guidance on the proper method of reporting expense reimbursements to employees engaged in lobbying activities on Form 650 lobbying disclosure reports, noting that three possible reporting methods exist but lack clear regulatory guidance.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC clarified that while all three previously-used reporting methods were considered acceptable, the proper method going forward is to report employee expense reimbursements in Section B, Part 1 as payments made outside the filer's organization, regardless of whether the employee spends 10% or more of their time on lobbying activities.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both incoming request (December 22, 1982 letter from Fred Lowell) and FPPC response (February 7, 1983 letter from Lynn Montgomery). The letter explicitly notes this does not constitute formal advice under Section 83114 because it was not requested on behalf of a specifically identified client. Extraction confidence high due to clear question presentation and detailed conclusion; minor uncertainty due to lack of formal 'QUESTION' and 'CONCLUSION' section headers.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How should a lobbyist employer report reimbursement of expenses paid to employees who spent 10% or more of their compensated time in attempts to influence legislative or administrative action? Should such expenses be reported in Section B(1) as payments to influence legislative action outside the organization, in Section B(3) as part of employee compensation, or in Section B(3) as overhead?

CONCLUSION: The Act is not specific regarding the proper method of reporting such payments. With regard to previous reporting, any of the three methods of reporting which you describe in your letter are considered to be adequate. The staff has recently determined, however, that the proper method as shown on page 20 of the "Information Manual on Lobbying Disclosure Provisions" of reporting reimbursement of expenses of employees who spend 10% or more of their time in lobbying activities is to report such payments in Section B, Part 1, "Payments Aggregating $25 or more outside the Filers Organization," since the costs were incurred outside of the filers organization. In addition to reporting reimbursement of expenses of employees who spend 10% or more of their time, reimbursement of expenses of employees who spend less than 10% of their time must also be reported pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18620(d)(C), in the same manner.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to a request for advice on reporting employee expense reimbursements by lobbyist-employers on Form 650, clarifying that such expenses should be reported in Section B, Part 1 as payments made outside the organization, with retroactive acceptance of three previously-used reporting methods.
